zotero.abstractNote: Second-order optimization methods for deep learning require approximations of the curvature matrix, such as the Fisher information matrix or the Hessian. A common strategy is to use block-diagonal approximations, where blocks correspond to layers or even individual parameters. We propose a modular framework for constructing such approximations. It decomposes the curvature of a feedforward network into local contributions from individual layers and activation functions. This modularity allows for a systematic derivation of various existing approximations, such as K-FAC, and facilitates the development of new ones. We demonstrate the flexibility of our framework by deriving a novel block-diagonal approximation that accounts for correlations between weights and biases, and show that it improves upon standard K-FAC on small-scale problems.
zotero.conferenceName: 23rd International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Statistics
zotero.proceedingsTitle: Proceedings of the 23rd International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Statistics
